title: Weekly Logistics Infrastructure Intelligence Report
executiveSummary: Terminal infrastructure remains a live institutional allocation theme, with the Stonepeak-CMA CGM platform still anchoring the hard-asset narrative for PE and infrastructure investors.
Recent U.S. federal port awards continue to reinforce that public capital is actively shaping which modernization programs move from planning into execution.
Private 5G and automation-heavy terminal deployments in the UK and Korea suggest the digital layer is being treated more like operating infrastructure than discretionary innovation spend.
Port Saint John remains one of the clearest examples of modernization spend translating into measurable throughput gains and network relevance.
European congestion, energy-related inland surcharges, and Gulf-linked disruption keep resilience, fuel pass-through, and execution discipline central to underwriting.

marketSignals.summary: This week’s highest-signal developments combine hard-asset capital allocation, fresh public funding visibility, automation buildout, and ongoing freight-network fragility. For investors, the message remains consistent: infrastructure and enabling systems are still attractive, but performance under disruption is now part of the core diligence lens.

rows.Headline: Stonepeak-CMA CGM terminal platform remains the benchmark capital signal
rows.Summary: Stonepeak is investing $2.4 billion for a 25% stake in United Ports LLC, the 10-terminal platform formed with CMA CGM.
rows.Why investors should pay attention: The transaction continues to validate institutional demand for scarce terminal assets with long-duration cash-flow potential.

rows.Headline: U.S. DOT port awards move modernization from intent to execution
rows.Summary: The U.S. Department of Transportation announced nearly $489 million across 31 port-related projects on March 26, 2026.
rows.Why investors should pay attention: Fresh awards create clearer execution pipelines and make local modernization activity more investable.

rows.Headline: Port Saint John continues to provide modernization proof
rows.Summary: DP World reported 29.4% container volume growth in 2025 at Port Saint John following the CAD$247 million modernization program.
rows.Why investors should pay attention: It remains a useful benchmark for how capex can convert into throughput growth and stronger network positioning.

rows.Headline: Incheon begins work on first fully automated terminal
rows.Summary: Incheon Port Authority confirmed superstructure construction for the Incheon New Port Phase 1-2 terminal, its first fully automated container terminal.
rows.Why investors should pay attention: It is a concrete signal that automation infrastructure is moving into large-scale physical buildout rather than remaining at pilot stage.

rows.Headline: Maersk flags continuing European network pressure in April update
rows.Summary: Maersk’s April Europe market update highlights yard density, weather disruption, and holiday-related labor constraints across North Europe and the Mediterranean.
rows.Why investors should pay attention: Congestion and labor-related instability remain relevant to asset utilization, turnaround times, and resilience-oriented capex.

capitalFlow.summary: Capital continues to cluster around infrastructure-grade logistics assets, modernization programs backed by public funding, and gateways with measurable operating leverage. The pattern remains one of public and private capital reinforcing each other rather than substituting for one another.

rows.Strategic implication: Reinforces terminal platforms as core infrastructure allocation targets.
Fresh federal awards support project sequencing and sharpen visibility into near-term U.S. port execution.
Maintains federal support for port and freight-corridor capex.

rows.Investor or buyer: Government of Canada, Province of New Brunswick, Port Saint John, DP World
rows.Amount if known: CAD$247M project; CAD$75M+ DP World investment
rows.Strategic implication: Demonstrates funded modernization with measurable throughput and connectivity outcomes.
State funding continues to accelerate infrastructure readiness and co-investment potential.

technologyDeployments.summary: Technology deployment now matters primarily as evidence of operational maturity. The strongest signals this week remain private 5G, automation buildout, and AI safety systems being implemented in environments where reliability, labor productivity, and coordination directly affect asset performance.

rows.Operator or sponsor: Three Group Solutions / Hutchison Ports UK
rows.Operational impact: Supports autonomous vehicles, remote cranes, and automation-heavy workflows across major UK port assets.
Builds a connectivity layer for AI, automation, and real-time coordination across logistics sites.
Pushes AI into preventive safety and incident reduction in terminal operations, with full system operation scheduled for August 2026.

rows.Deployment: Fully automated terminal construction phase begins
rows.Technology: Remote unmanned terminal handling and automation infrastructure
rows.Operational impact: Signals physical buildout of automation-ready terminal infrastructure, including equipment rails, yard paving, and power systems.

risks.summary: Disruption risk remains broad rather than isolated. For infrastructure investors, that matters because it affects terminal utilization, labor planning, inland transport economics, bunker pass-through, and the value of resilience-oriented upgrades.

rows.Event: Yard density, weather disruption, and Easter-related labor pressure
rows.Potential logistics impact: Longer dwell times, delayed cargo clearance, and schedule instability across European port and inland networks.

rows.Event: Emergency fuel surcharge across all trades
rows.Potential logistics impact: Higher fuel-related freight costs and greater pass-through volatility, with FMC-scope application starting April 8, 2026.

rows.Event: North Europe inland fuel and energy surcharges
rows.Potential logistics impact: Raises inland transport and handling costs across truck, waterway, and combined-rail modes.
Projects lacking permitting and execution readiness may still miss near-term public-capital windows despite available awards and programs.

narratives.summary: The investor narrative is tightening around two ideas: first, capital still prefers infrastructure with hard operating relevance; second, technology must now prove that it can improve performance in live environments. That continues to support a PE and infrastructure-focused briefing rather than a VC-style innovation narrative.

rows.Narrative theme: Terminal infrastructure remains a preferred allocation
rows.Why investors care: Scarce gateway assets continue to attract large-scale institutional capital.

rows.Narrative theme: Public funding is still steering modernization timing
rows.Supporting signals: USDOT awards, NYSERDA, PIDP, Saint John
rows.Why investors care: Funding windows and public-private alignment are still driving execution order.

rows.Narrative theme: Private 5G is becoming a core operating layer
rows.Supporting signals: Hutchison UK and Thames Freeport deployments
rows.Why investors care: Connectivity is increasingly tied to automation and reliability, not experimentation.

rows.Narrative theme: AI diligence has shifted to post-pilot execution
rows.Supporting signals: Busan rollout, Incheon automation buildout, and pilot-to-scale critique
rows.Why investors care: Investors care whether AI and automation survive integration into live operating systems.

rows.Narrative theme: Disruption resilience remains part of core underwriting
rows.Supporting signals: Maersk Europe update and Hapag-Lloyd fuel and war-related surcharges
rows.Why investors care: Freight instability continues to affect throughput, cost, and infrastructure value capture.

andrewLinkedInPost.title: Why operating proof matters more than modernization intent
andrewLinkedInPost.body: One of the clearest logistics infrastructure signals this week is that the market is rewarding operating proof, not just modernization intent. Fresh U.S. port awards reinforce that public capital is still moving, but the more important question is where that capital is producing durable operating outcomes. Port Saint John remains a useful benchmark because modernization has already started to show up in throughput. At the same time, private 5G deployments across UK ports and construction on Incheon’s first fully automated terminal show that automation infrastructure is moving from concept into physical operating environments. The key diligence question for PE and infrastructure investors is less about who has the most technology and more about which assets, networks, and operating models are converting capital into reliable performance under real-world strain.

quaychainPost.title: This week’s infrastructure signal
quaychainPost.body: This week’s logistics infrastructure picture remains clear: terminal capital is still active, public port awards are moving projects closer to execution, private 5G is being treated more like operating infrastructure, and completed upgrades such as Port Saint John are showing measurable throughput gains. At the same time, European congestion and fuel-related surcharges continue to keep resilience central to underwriting. For investors and infrastructure advisors, the relevant filter is practical: which assets can convert capital into reliable operating performance under real-world pressure?

engagementTopics.summary: The strongest discussion areas this week are the ones where Andrew can add an infrastructure and execution lens rather than a vendor narrative.

rows.Why it matters: Investors are still discussing terminal valuation, control, and asset scarcity.
rows.Suggested comment: This still looks like an asset-quality and strategic-location story, with institutional capital following infrastructure that is difficult to replicate.

rows.Topic: Port modernization with measurable throughput outcomes
rows.Why it matters: Saint John remains a useful operating benchmark after major modernization spend.
rows.Suggested comment: The strongest modernization signals are the ones that show up in volume growth, service reliability, and network relevance rather than in project language alone.

rows.Why it matters: Investors remain wary of deployments that do not survive production conditions.
rows.Suggested comment: Pilot success matters less than whether deployment can integrate into workflows and continue producing value under live operating pressure.

communities.summary: Public LinkedIn communities and pages remain the most reliable channels for investor-adjacent visibility.

rows.Why relevant: Strong supply chain and infrastructure audience with analyst overlap.
Useful freight-market and pricing discussion with investor relevance.
Broad logistics audience that still captures capital-flow and modernization discussion.

outreachTargets.summary: These remain the most relevant investor and analyst targets for this week’s signal set.

rows.Name: James Wyper
rows.Why relevant: Closely tied to the lead infrastructure-capital transaction.

rows.Name: Lars Jensen
rows.Why relevant: High-signal perspective on shipping economics and network disruption.

rows.Name: Peter Sand
rows.Why relevant: Relevant for freight-rate, surcharge, and corridor-risk interpretation.

newsletterIdea.title: From port awards to operating proof in logistics infrastructure
newsletterIdea.description: A weekly briefing connecting terminal capital, fresh U.S. port awards, private 5G deployment, and live evidence of modernization translating into throughput and resilience.
newsletterIdea.whyInvestorsShouldRead: It keeps the focus on durable infrastructure outcomes and post-rollout execution quality rather than announcement flow.

actions: Engage in terminal JV, port modernization, and AI pilot-to-scale discussion threads.
Comment once in a LinkedIn community from the target list.
Publish a short X thread on why operating proof matters more than modernization announcements alone.
Send outreach to 2-3 investor and analyst targets with a message focused on durable operating performance.
